coredata.dc:title: Advanced flexible photocatalytic fuel cell using TiO2/carbon quantum dots photoanode for green electricity production
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shijie
Tang, Jixia
Chen, Weiyu
Qian, Tu
Li, Xuechen
Feng, Zixuan
He, Jie
Zhang, Rui
Yang, Zhengchun
Li, Huayi
Pan, Peng
Zhang, Kailiang
Zheng, Lingcheng
Feng, Deqiang
coredata.dc:description: Flexible electronics have attracted great attention in recent years. However, the matching power sources of these flexible electronics now are mostly rigid, greatly hindering the way of all-flexible electronics. Here, a flexible photocatalytic fuel cell (fPFC) was designed to produce a stable energy output (highest power density of 40.1 mW·cm−2·g−1 in artificial sweat) while purify wasted water simultaneously. The fPFC is consisted of TiO2/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) photoanode and Ag cathode. The experimental results show that the combination of TiO2 and CQDs can broaden the range of light absorption of TiO2. Thus, the photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC can reach up to 252.3 mA·cm−2·g−1, while the photocurrent density of pure TiO2 fPFC is 87.4 mA·cm−2·g−1. The photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC is 188.7% higher than that of TiO2 fPFC. The maximum power density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in flatting state is about 40.1 mW·cm−2·g−1. This TiO2/CQDs fPFC has better performance than fPFC in other literature. Besides, TiO2/CQDs fPFC in the bending state can also achieve a good performance. Four fPFCs can be easily constructed into a bracelet and generate electricity, and there is no significant change in opening voltage under flatting and bending states. This eco-friendly system has great potential in energy conservation and portable electronics, and can be applied in the following aspects: (i) wearable technology that utilizes organic waste for power generation; (ii) flexible configuration of traditional sewage treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
TiO2
Oxidation of organic waste
Flexible photocatalytic fuel cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of flexible photocatalytic fuel cell
PEC properties of TiO2 fPFC and TiO2/CQDs fPFC
The electrochemical reaction mechanism of TiO2/CQDs fPFC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs (a) XRD patterns; (b) Ti 2p XPS spectra; (c) O 1 s XPS spectra; (d) C 1 s XPS spectra.
SEM images of (a) TiO2 and (b) TiO2/CQDs; TEM images of (c) TiO2 and (d) TiO2/CQDs; and the insets in c and d are SAED patterns of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs.
(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectra and (b) Tauc plot of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs photoanodes.

Transient photocurrent response (a) TiO2, TiO2/CQDs and (b) TiO2 fPFC, TiO2/CQDs fPFC in flatting state; and (c) polarization curves and power density curves, (d) EIS curves of TiO2 fPFC and TiO2/CQDs fPFC in flatting state.

The bending TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs fPFC: (a)bending picture; (b) transient photocurrent response; (c) EIS curves; (d) the opening voltage of TiO2/CQDs fPFCs in flatting state and bending state respectively.
(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectra and (b) photodegradation curves of TiO2/CQDs fPFC.
(a) Band diagram of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs and (b) Schematic model for the important roles of CQDs for TiO2/CQDs.
Schematic diagram of organic oxidation reaction in TiO2/CQDs fPFC.
The positions of the TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs energy levels.

head.ce:title: Advanced flexible photocatalytic fuel cell using TiO2/carbon quantum dots photoanode for green electricity production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering
Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanics and Photoelectric Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, China

Anhui University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Qian Xuesen Laboratory of Space Technology, China Academy of Space Technology, Beijing 100094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Qian Xuesen Laboratory of Space Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Qian Xuesen Laboratory of Space Technology, China Academy of Space Technology, Beijing 100094, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible electronics have attracted great attention in recent years. However, the matching power sources of these flexible electronics now are mostly rigid, greatly hindering the way of all-flexible electronics. Here, a flexible photocatalytic fuel cell (fPFC) was designed to produce a stable energy output (highest power density of 40.1 mW·cm−2·g−1 in artificial sweat) while purify wasted water simultaneously. The fPFC is consisted of TiO2/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) photoanode and Ag cathode. The experimental results show that the combination of TiO2 and CQDs can broaden the range of light absorption of TiO2. Thus, the photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC can reach up to 252.3 mA·cm−2·g−1, while the photocurrent density of pure TiO2 fPFC is 87.4 mA·cm−2·g−1. The photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC is 188.7% higher than that of TiO2 fPFC. The maximum power density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in flatting state is about 40.1 mW·cm−2·g−1. This TiO2/CQDs fPFC has better performance than fPFC in other literature. Besides, TiO2/CQDs fPFC in the bending state can also achieve a good performance. Four fPFCs can be easily constructed into a bracelet and generate electricity, and there is no significant change in opening voltage under flatting and bending states. This eco-friendly system has great potential in energy conservation and portable electronics, and can be applied in the following aspects: (i) wearable technology that utilizes organic waste for power generation; (ii) flexible configuration of traditional sewage treatment.

A flexible photocatalytic fuel cell (fPFC) based on TiO2/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) photoanode has been fabricated for treating organic waste and efficiently generating electricity. The bracelet made by four bending fPFCs in series still has a high photocurrent density and power density.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advent of global industrialization and the over-exploitation of energy, energy crisis and man-made organic waste was on the rise [1–3]. At present, people are studying various energy conversion devices to solve the above problems. These include nanogenerators that convert mechanical energy into electricity, solar cells that convert light energy into electricity [4,5], thermoelectric devices that convert heat energy into electricity [6], biofuel cells that convert bioenergy into electricity, and a number of other technologies [7,8]. However, the above solutions can not take into account the problem of man-made organic waste while saving non-renewable energy.

The photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) is a promising option that combines photocatalysis and fuel cell technologies. It can be used to treat organic waste or biological waste [9–11]. Therefore, PFC can not only achieve resource recycling, but also generate electricity. Through this innovative technology, there is an opportunity to solve environmental pollution problems and promote the development of clean energy [12–14]. Unlike conventional fuel cells, which use precious metals as anode catalysts, typical PFC employ semiconductors such as TiO2 as photoanode catalysts, greatly reducing costs [15–17]. When the semiconductor photocatalyst is illuminated by light, electrons in the valence band (VB) will be excited to the conduction band (CB), and photogenerated holes can be produced in the VB. These holes have strong oxidation properties and can degrade various organic waste. At the same time, photogenerated electrons are transferred to the cathode of the PFC, producing electricity [11,18]. As a result, the PFC can achieve the degradation of organic waste and generation of energy. However, at present, most PFCs are prepared based on microfluidic structures, which can effectively increase the reaction area. However, the preliminary design and preparation of the microfluidic structure are complicated, and still require continuous external fuel supply [19,20]. Besides, the integrated PFC is also constructed by connecting several PFCs in series in an electrolytic cell. The efficiency of this structure is low because the cathode of this PFC is immersed in the electrolyte and can't fully contact with the air. Therefore, this integrated PFC also requires an external oxygen supply device to continuously supply oxygen to the cathode, and the overall structure is relatively complex [21,22]. Besides, the volume of this integrated PFC is also large.

Recent developments in portable and flexible electronics have pushed our life closer to a new era filled with wearable products. However, cells that matches these flexible electronics are still some way off. Until now, the most reported cells are lithium-ion cells, and the relatively emerging lithium sulfur (Li-S) cells. However, lithium-ion cells are prone to thermal runaway, leading to fires and explosions, and the fixed geometry and size of conventional rechargeable Li-S cells have largely limited their application [23]. In addition, zinc-air cells have also been extensively studied, but their development has been limited by their low energy efficiency [23]. Furthermore, these cells exhibit rigidity in their structure due to a series of reasons such as strong material rigidity, liquid electrolyte leakage, and packaging material limitations [24]. These questions limit the above cells widespread use in practical applications, and seriously hinder the way of all-flexible electronics. Flexible photocatalytic fuel cells (fPFCs) can be considered a promising breakthrough due to the following reasons [25–27]: firstly, the fuels used are non-selective and can be ethanol, glucose, organic waste, or even pollutants. Therefore, fPFCs can degrade pollutants while generating electricity. Secondly, the electrodes of fPFCs can be made of carbon cloth, plastic film and other materials with good flexibility, which is conducive to the development of all-flexible of fPFCs. In addition, fPFCs do not require a continuous fuel supply or external oxygen supply device, so the structure of fPFCs is relatively simple and easy to prepare. fPFCs can sustainably supply energy for all kinds of flexible electronics because of the uninterrupted energy collection and store mode [14,22,28,29].

TiO2 is a n-type semiconductor with a wide band gap (
E g
) of 3.4 eV, which is widely used in organic waste degradation, wastewater treatment, power generation and other fields [17,30–32]. However, pure TiO2 has a large
E g
and can only absorb ultraviolet light. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm and have unique photoluminescence and photoelectron transfer properties [33]. Therefore, combining CQDs with other metal oxide semiconductors can be considered as an excellent strategy for designing high-efficiency photocatalysts [34]. In this work, TiO2 doped with CQDs (TiO2/CQDs) was spined onto indium tin oxide (ITO)/polyethylene terephthalate (PET) film (ITO/PET) as the photoanode of fPFC. TiO2/CQDs can reduce the
E g
of TiO2 and improve the light absorption capacity of TiO2, thus promoting the photocatalytic performance. Ag on carbon cloth was used as the cathode. The waterproof breathable layer was used to protect the cathode and is conducive to the absorption of oxygen by the cathode. Cosmetic cotton paper was used as flexible electrolyte membrane. The fPFC is hot melt packaged with a light permeable film, and this package can replace the fuel in the fPFC at any time. This fPFC can achieve the degradation of organic waste and the generation of efficient power at the same time. The fPFC can also be bent into a bracelet to power electronic devices.

Absolute ethanol was provided by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. TiO2 (20 nm, Anatase phase) and titanium (IV) isopropoxide were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. ITO/PET (5 Ω/cm2) was provided by Luoyang Guluo Glass Co., Ltd. Carbon cloth (W0S1011) was provided by Beijing Jingke Instrument Science Instruments Co., Ltd. Conductive silver paste was provided by Beijing Nano Top Electronic Technology Co., Ltd. Artificial sweat (0.031 mol/L NaCl, 0.014 mol/L lactic acid, 0.01 mol/L urea, 1.7 × 10−4 mol/L glucose) was provided by Dongguan Chuangfeng Automation Technology Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA, 99+ %) was provided by Thermo Fisher Scientific. Urea was provided by Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. All other reagents were of analytical grades and used without further purification.

100 g of CA and 100 g of urea were dissolved in 1 L of deionized water and transferred to a glass beaker. The resulting solution was then subjected to microwave irradiation with a potency of 700 W for 5 min, by using a domestic microwave. After the 5 min, 1 L of deionized water were added to re-suspend the resulting precipitate in water. Synthesized CQDs were subsequently centrifuged (in reusable centrifuge tubes) at 6000 rpm during 20 min in order to eliminate suspended impurities [35,36].

0.95 mL ethanol, 0.05 mL titanium isopropanol (IV), and 100 mg TiO2 were mixed, sonicated for 30 min, and then spined onto ITO/PET (low speed: 200 rpm, 9 s; high speed: 500 rpm, 10 s). Then, the prepared film was heat-treated at 120 °C for 1 h with a heating up speed of 5 °C/min. Finally, TiO2 photoanode is obtained.
100 mg TiO2 was added to the prepared 5 mL CQDs solution, stirred for 10 min, and then the mixed solution was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain TiO2/CQDs nanocomposites.

100 mg TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite was added to the mixture of 0.95 mL ethanol and 0.05 mL titanium (IV) isopropoxide, treated with ultrasound for 45 min, and then the slurry was spined onto the ITO/PET (low speed: 200 rpm, 9 s; high speed: 500 rpm, 10 s), and then the prepared photoanode was heat treated at 140 °C for 1 h with a heating up speed of 5 °C/min. Finally, TiO2/CQDs photoanode is obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of flexible photocatalytic fuel cell
ce:section.ce:para: The structure diagram is shown in (Fig. 1 ). The silver paste was printed on carbon cloth by screen printing method as a flexible cathode. TiO2 or TiO2/CQDs was spined onto ITO/PET by hydrothermal synthesis method as a photoanode. As an electrolyte membrane, the cosmetic cotton is located between the photoanode and the cathode. The hot melt encapsulation of the cell was carried out by using the flexible light permeable film. It is worth noting that part of the light permeable film above the cathode is replaced by a waterproof breathable layer in order to facilitate the absorption of oxygen. Artificial sweat is used as fuel. The fPFC with TiO2 as the photoanode is called TiO2 fPFC for short, and the fPFC with TiO2/CQDs as the photoanode is called TiO2/CQDs fPFC for short.

The crystalline structure of the prepared photoanode material was characterized by X-ray diffractometry (XRD; Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan). The morphologies of photoanodes were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM; Verios 460 L, FEI, USA) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM; Talos F200 X, FEI, USA). The compositions of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs were measured by ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, UV-5100B, Metash, China).

PEC testing of the photoanode and the fPFC was done by the electrochemical workstation (VersaSTAT 3, Princeton, USA). The light source was UV light with a power density of 83 mW/cm2, which was provided by xenon lamp with a filter (UVCUT400). The electrolyte is artificial sweat.

The PEC test of photoelectrode adopts the three-electrode method. The working electrode (WE) was TiO2 or TiO2/CQDs growing on the ITO/PET film, the counter electrode (CE) was the Pt wire, and the reference electrode (RE) was Ag/AgCl (3.5 mol/L KCl). Under interval illumination, transient current response tests were carried out under the condition of constant voltage of 0 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed in the voltage range − 1–1 V vs. RHE. When the photocurrent density is 10 μA·cm−2, the corresponding voltage is defined as the opening voltage.

The PEC test of fPFC adopts the two-electrode method. Photocurrent measurements are made without any applied bias or current. Polarization curves were measured using current dynamics (100 μA/s). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured in the frequency range of 1 Hz – 1 MHz. The opening voltage of TiO2 fPFCs and TiO2/CQDs fPFCs were measured by digit dual display multimeter (U3402A, Agilent, Malaysia).

By photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds, the photocatalytic performance of TiO2/CQDs fPFC and its impact on environmental governance were further tested. Methylene blue (MB) solution is one of the sources of serious water pollution. Therefore, In the photodegradation experiment, 40 mL MB solution with a concentration of 10 mg/L was used as an organic pollutant. The light source was UV light with a power density of 83 mW/cm2. At 0.5 h, 1 h, 1.5 h, 2 h, 2.5 h, 3 h and 3.5 h, 3 mL MB solution was used to measure its absorption spectrum by UV-5100B UV–visible spectrophotometer. The degradation rate of MB by TiO2/CQDs fPFC is calculated, and the degradation rate of MB can be expressed as follows:
(1)
D =
C 0
− C
/
C 0
× 100 % =
A 0
−
A t
/
A 0
× 100 %
where D is the degradation rate, C is the concentration of MB, A0 is the absorbance of the original MB solution before photodegradation, and At is the absorbance of MB solution after t time of photodegradation.

Fig. 2a shows the XRD patterns of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs. It can be seen that the diffraction peaks are 25.30° (101), 36.88° (103), 37.66° (004), 38.54° (112), 48.06° (200), 53.74° (105), 55.08° (211), 62.62° (204), 68.58° (116), 70.30° (220), 74.94° (215), 76.06° (301). It is consistent with the standard spectrum of anatase (JCPDS 2–387), indicating that TiO2 is anatase phase. No difference between TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs was found in XRD pattern. In order to prove the successful synthesis of TiO2/CQDs, the XPS spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs were respectively tested, as shown in (Fig. 2b-d). Fig. S1 shows the presence of Ti 2p, O 1 s, C 1 s in TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs [37]. Fig. 2b shows that there are two main peaks of 458.6 eV and 464.3 eV for TiO2, corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, respectively, indicating that TiO2 exists in pure Ti4+ oxidation state [38,39]. The spectrum of O 1 s in TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs is shown in (Fig. 2c). There are two main peaks of O 1 s in TiO2, 529.8 eV and 531.3 eV, corresponding to Ti-O and Ti-OH respectively [39,40]. As shown in (Fig. 2d), the peaks of C 1 s corresponding to 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.8 eV in TiO2 are C-C, C-O and O-C-Ti, respectively [41]. For TiO2/CQDs, the binding energy of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 is basically unchanged compared with TiO2. The main peaks of O 1 s in TiO2/CQDs are 530.0 eV and 531.7 eV, respectively, and the binding energy increases by 0.2 eV and 0.4 eV compared with TiO2, which may be due to the new binding between O and C of Ti-O [39,40]. The C 1 s peaks in TiO2/CQDs are 285.8 eV and 288.6 eV respectively, and their binding energy is reduced by 0.1 eV and 0.2 eV compared with TiO2. This result indicates that the introduction of CQDs increases the electron cloud density around C [41].

The SEM images of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs are shown in (Fig. 3a and b). The particle size of TiO2 is about 21 nm. As shown in Fig. 3b, the morphology of TiO2 has no obvious change when combing with CQDs. To further prove the successful composite of TiO2 and CQDs, HRTEM can be used to analyze lattice constants of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs. As can be seen from (Fig. 3c and d), the lattice spacing is 0.35 nm, which is the (101) crystal plane of TiO2, indicating the existence of anatase phase TiO2 [37]. Fig. 3d clearly shows the uniform distribution of CQDs with particle size of about 3 nm on TiO2. The insets in (Fig. 3c and d) show the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs. The corresponding d-spacings are (101), (004), (200), (211) and (204), which proves again that the TiO2 exists is anatase phase [42]. These results demonstrate the successful preparation of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs.

The optical properties of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs were analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fig. 4a shows the UV–Vis spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs photoanodes. As can be seen from Fig. 4a, TiO2 can only absorb light with wavelengths lower than 364 nm, while the absorption edge of TiO2/CQDs extends to 538 nm. There are two main reasons for the expansion of the absorption range after the introduction of CQDs: firstly, CQDs can absorb visible light, so the combination of CQDs and TiO2 improves the light absorption capacity of the whole system. Secondly, the up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) property of CQDs makes it emit shorter wavelength light (300–530 nm) after absorbing the light, which in turn excites TiO2 to form electron-hole pairs. Therefore, TiO2/CQDs can greatly improve the utilization capacity of solar energy compared with pure TiO2 and ultimately expand the light response range, thereby improving photocatalytic performance [43].
E g
of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs can be estimated by Tauc plot, which can be determined by the following formula:
(2)
αhv
2
= A
hv −
E g
(3)
hv = h
c λ
where
α is the absorption coefficient, h (6.626 × 10−34 J · Hz−1) is the Planck constant, v
is the illumination frequency,
A is the constant, c
(3 × 108 m · s−1) is the speed of light, and λ
is the wavelength. As shown in (Fig. 4b),
E g
of TiO2/CQDs is reduced to 2.8 eV, demonstrating that
E g
of the photoanode can be reduced by combining with CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC properties of TiO2 fPFC and TiO2/CQDs fPFC
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the PEC properties of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs photoanodes, the transient photocurrent response and LSV were measured. As shown in (Fig. 5a), the transient photocurrent density of pure TiO2 photoanode is 0.025 mA·cm−2 and that of TiO2/CQDs photoanode is 0.038 mA·cm−2. The photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs photoanode is 52% higher than that of TiO2 photoanode. This is mainly because the addition of CQDs can enhance the optical absorption range of TiO2/CQDs system, narrow the
E g
, and generate more photogenerated carriers. LSV curves of TiO2 photoanode are shown in (Fig. S2), the opening voltage of TiO2 photoanode is −0.30 V. LSV curves of TiO2/CQDs photoanode are shown in (Fig. S3), the opening voltage of TiO2/CQDs photoanode is −0.80 V, which is more negative than that of pure TiO2. A more negative opening voltage is favorable for photocatalysis, which further proves that CQDs can improve the performance of photocatalysis.

The transient photocurrent response of TiO2 fPFC and TiO2/CQDs fPFC are shown in (Fig. 5b). The photocurrent density of TiO2 fPFC and TiO2/CQDs fPFC in flatting state are 87.4 mA·cm−2·g−1 and 252.3 mA·cm−2·g−1 respectively. The photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC is 188.7% higher than that of TiO2 fPFC. Due to the constant consumption of artificial sweat, its photocurrent density shows a slight downward trend. The polarization curves and power density curves of TiO2 fPFC and TiO2/CQDs fPFC at the flatting state are shown in (Fig. 5c), the open circuit voltage (
V OC
) and short circuit current density (
J SC
) are the vertical and horizontal intercepts of the polarization curves respectively. The
V OC
of TiO2 fPFC and TiO2/CQDs fPFC are 0.65 V and 0.57 V, respectively. The
J SC
of TiO2 fPFC and TiO2/CQDs fPFC are 80.2 mA·cm−2·g−1 and 249.6 mA·cm−2·g−1, respectively. The maximum power density (
P max
) of TiO2 fPFC in flatting state can be calculated as 13.0 mW·cm−2·g−1, and the
P max
of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in flatting state is about 40.1 mW·cm−2·g−1. The above results show that the introduction of CQDs greatly improves the photocatalytic performance of fPFC.

Next, electron properties of TiO2/CQDs fPFC and TiO2 fPFC are tested using EIS. EIS reflects the reaction rate at the electrode interface. The smaller the radius of the Nyquist semicircle, the better the separation effect of electron-hole pairs, indicating a lower carrier transfer resistance (Rct) [44]. As can be seen from (Fig. 5d), under the light condition, the Nyquist semicircle radius of TiO2 fPFC decreases. The results show that under the condition of light, it is advantageous to the separation of electron-hole pairs. The introduction of CQDs also leads to a significantly decreased radius of the semicircular Nyquist plot as compared with TiO2 fPFC, providing strong supports for the effective separation of electron-hole pairs after adding CQDs [45].

As shown in Table S1, the performance of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in this work is compared with that in other literatures, and the filling factor ( FF ) is calculated as follows:
(4)
FF =
P max
V OC
×
J SC

According to the above formula, the FF of the TiO2/CQDs fPFC prepared in this work is 28.1%. Comparing with other literatures, it is found that the external light intensity has a great influence on the performance. However, the TiO2/CQDs fPFC prepared in this work still has a higher
P max
and FF with a smaller light intensity.

In order to explore the potential of TiO2/CQDs fPFC with flexibility, the PEC properties of TiO2/CQDs fPFC under bending were tested. As shown in (Fig. 6b), the photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC with a radius of curvature of 2.5 cm (Fig. 6a) is 135.1 mA·cm−2·g−1. Because the cosmetic cotton may be separated from electrodes during bending, the fuel in cosmetic cotton may be not fully contact with electrodes, and the photocurrent density of this fPFC slightly decreases in bending state. However, the photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in bending is still 54.2% higher than that of the flatting TiO2 fPFC. EIS curves of TiO2/CQDs fPFC under bending condition are shown in (Fig. 6c). In the bending state, the impedance of TiO2/CQDs fPFC is significantly reduced in the light than that in the dark, which indicates that charge transfer is dominant during illumination. Under the light condition, the EIS curve of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in bending has little change compared to that in flatting state, showing that carriers can also be separated quickly for the bending TiO2/CQDs fPFC. Fig. S4 shows the power density curve of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in the bending state, and the
P max
is 18.5 mW·cm−2·g−1. The opening voltage of TiO2/CQDs fPFCs in flatting and bending state is shown in (Fig. 6d). When exposed to UV light in the flatting state, four flatting TiO2/CQDs fPFCs in series can generate the voltage of about 1.94 V, and the generated voltage is about 1.71 V when these four TiO2/CQDs fPFCs are connected in series and bent into a bracelet. Compared with other works (as shown in Table S1), the
V OC
,
J SC
and
P max
of the TiO2/CQDs fPFC prepared in this work were compared with other TiO2 fPFC, microbial fuel cell, and TiO2 PFC. The results indicate that TiO2/CQDs fPFC in this work has better performance compared to other work [46].

In order to evaluate the potential capacity of TiO2/CQDs fPFC to generate electricity from organic waste, the PEC performance of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in flatting state with methylene blue (MB, 10 mg/L) solution as the electrolyte was also tested. The transient photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC with MB as electrolyte is shown in (Fig. S5a). The photocurrent density of TiO2/CQDs fPFC is 24.1 mA·cm−2·g−1. LSV curve is shown in (Fig. S5b), and the opening voltage is about 0.4 V. The polarization curve and power density curve of TiO2/CQDs fPFC are shown in (Fig. S5c). The
V OC
is 0.48 V, the
J SC
is 23.85 mA·cm−2·g−1, and the
P max
is 4.81 mW·cm−2·g−1. The EIS curve of TiO2/CQDs fPFC is shown in (Fig. S5d). It can be seen that the radius of TiO2/CQDs fPFC EIS curve is significantly reduced under light conditions compared with that under dark conditions, indicating that the Rct of TiO2/CQDs fPFC is significantly reduced. The comprehensive data is lower than that of TiO2/CQDs fPFC with artificial sweat as electrolyte, and the efficiency of TiO2/CQDs fPFC with electrolyte as artificial sweat biological waste is higher.

Due to MB solution is one of the sources of serious water pollution. In order to verify the treatment effect of TiO2/CQDs fPFC on environmental pollution, the degradation performance of TiO2/CQDs fPFC on MB solution was tested. Fig. 7a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of TiO2/CQDs fPFC in MB solution under light conditions. The results showed that in the range of 500 nm to 800 nm, MB exhibited two major absorption peaks at 612 nm and 663 nm, respectively. These two peaks represent the characteristic absorption wavelength of MB, with 663 nm being its maximum absorption wavelength. With the increase of irradiation time, the peak absorbance of MB at 663 nm continuously decreases. After 3.5 h irradiation, the peak absorbance decreased from 1.7 to 0.8, which indicates that TiO2/CQDs fPFC can effectively degrade MB solution, and the longer the time, the more fully the degradation. Fig. 7b shows the degradation curve of MB solution under irradiation. It can be seen that the degradation rate of MB solution is 53% after 3.5 h. The experimental results show that TiO2/CQDs fPFC has a good ability to degrade the pollutants. It is conducive to environmental protection and clean energy development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The electrochemical reaction mechanism of TiO2/CQDs fPFC
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to further explore the reason for the improved performance of TiO2/CQDs fPFC, the valence band spectra and Mott-Schottky (M-S) curves were used to study the energy band structure of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs. The M-S curves of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs were shown in (Fig. S6). The intersection of the tangent line of the M-S curves of 1 kHz, 4 kHz and 10 kHz is the position of the Fermi level (
E F
). Therefore, the
E F
of TiO2 in pure TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs is −0.06 V and − 0.14 V, respectively. The valence band spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs are shown in (Fig. S7), which indicates the distance between the VB of the semiconductor and the
E F
. The distance between the VB of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs and their respective
E F
is 2.6 eV. The calculated valence band energy level (
E V
) of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs are 2.54 V and 2.46 V, respectively. Then the conduction band energy level (
E C
) can be obtained by the following formula:
(5)
E C
=
E V
−
E g

The results of calculating the positions of each energy level are shown in Table 1 . The corresponding band diagram is shown in (Fig. 8a). It is worth noting that the CB of TiO2/CQDs is lower than that of TiO2, while the VB of TiO2/CQDs is higher than that of TiO2. In the photocatalytic system, the higher the CB, the more likely reduction reaction occurs, and the lower the VB, the more likely oxidation reaction occurs [33]. But the experimental results show that TiO2/CQDs fPFC has better photocatalytic performance than TiO2 fPFC. There are mainly the following three reasons [47,48]: firstly, the introduction of CQDs reduces the
E g
of TiO2. At the same time, after the introduction of CQDs, the VB structure of TiO2 is disordered and the impurity energy level is introduced, which makes TiO2/CQDs easier to stimulate photogenerated electron-hole pairs. When pure TiO2 is irradiated by sunlight, electrons in the VB of TiO2 are excited and move to the CB. Because TiO2 has a wide
E g
(3.4 eV) and can only absorb light with shorter wavelengths (lower than 364 nm). However, TiO2/CQDs have a narrower
E g
(2.8 eV), which reduces the energy required to excite electrons. Therefore, under light conditions, TiO2/CQDs has a wider range of light absorption (lower than 538 nm). At the same time, more photogenerated carriers are produced [49]. After CQDs are coupled with TiO2, the presence of CQDs enhances the adsorption ability of TiO2/CQDs to organic molecules. The increase of adsorption capacity is conducive to the enrichment of the target reactants from the solution to the surface of the photocatalyst, further improving the photoredox activity [50,51]. Secondly, according to the XPS diagram analysis of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs, chemical bonds are formed between CQDs and TiO2, and the photogenerated electrons of such bonds are transferred to CQDs, which promotes the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thus improving the photocatalytic ability. Thirdly, CQDs can absorb visible light, produce photogenerated carriers, and the UCPL property of CQDs makes it emit shorter wavelength light (300–530 nm). This in turn excites TiO2 to form electron-hole pairs as shown in (Fig. 8b). The UCPL of CQDs greatly improves the utilization ability of TiO2/CQDs to sunlight, thereby expanding its photo response range. The photocatalytic performance of TiO2/CQDs was improved.

The possible photochemical reactions of glucose, lactic acid and urea in artificial sweat in TiO2/CQDs fPFC can be illustrated as follows. For TiO2/CQDs fPFC, the electrons in the VB of TiO2 absorb photon energy ( hv ), and are excited to the CB, producing photogenerated electrons (
e −
). And photogenerated holes (
h +
) are produced in the VB.
h +
has strong oxidation capacity, and its oxidation equation with glucose, lactic acid and urea is as follows:

For the photoanode:
(6)
Ti
O 2
/ CQDs + hv →
e −
+
h +

For glucose [52]:
(7)
C 6
H 12
O 6
+
O 2
+ 2
h +
→
C 6
H 10
O 8
+ 2
H +

For lactic acid [53]:
(8)
C
H 3
CH
OH
COOH + 2
h +
→ C
H 3
CHO +
CO 2
+ 2
H +

For urea [54]:
(9)
CO
N
H 2
2
+
O 2
+ 2
h +
→
CO 2
+
N 2
+
H 2
O + 2
H +

For the cathode:
(10)
4
H +
+
O 2
+ 4
e −
→ 2
H 2
O

According to the above formula, the diagram of photocatalytic reaction in TiO2/CQDs fPFC is shown in (Fig. 9 ). In the figure, OW is the abbreviation of organic waste before oxidation, and OW’ is the abbreviation of organic waste after oxidation. Under UV light, the photoanode produces photogenerated electron-hole pairs.
H +
is produced during the oxidation of OW by photogenerated holes. The photogenerated electrons are transferred to the cathode to undergo a reduction reaction with oxygen, and the reaction process generates water.

According to the analysis and discussion of the above experimental results, TiO2/CQDs fPFCs has a good application prospect. However, in practical applications, there are still some unresolved issues with our cells. For example, the current cathode is a silver paste coated on carbon cloth, which is relatively expensive. In future research, non-precious metals such as Cu, Mg, alkali metals, etc. can be attempted as substitutes. Besides, the photocurrent density and power density generated by fPFC are still too low compared to conventional lithium batteries [55–58].

In this work, a fPFC with TiO2/CQDs photoanode and Ag cathode was designed. The fuel is an artificial sweat containing glucose, lactic acid and urea. The experimental results show that the introduction of CQDs can broaden the light absorption range of TiO2, and the
E g
of TiO2 can be reduced to be 2.8 eV. The fPFCs can generate high photocurrent density and high
P max
(252.3 mA·cm−2·g−1, 40.1 mW·cm−2·g−1). In addition, four fPFCs can be easily constructed into a bracelet and generate electricity, the opening voltage of the bending fPFC has only slightly decreased compared with that in the flatting state, indicating a good flexibility of this TiO2/CQDs fPFC. This work provides a new idea for the preparation of the fPFC with the ability to treat organic matter and generate electricity.

Shijie Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jixia Tang: Visualization, Methodology. Weiyu Chen: Methodology. Tu Qian: Methodology. Xuechen Li: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Zixuan Feng: Methodology. Jie He: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Rui Zhang: Software, Methodology. Zhengchun Yang: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Huayi Li: Resources. Peng Pan: Resources. Kailiang Zhang: Resources. Lingcheng Zheng: Visualization. Deqiang Feng: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.
